narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream despite challenges like your low family income bracket, and we appreciate the courage it takes to get this far. The admissions model evaluated your profile by predicting your likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, and unfortunately, it estimated a 95% chance that you would not pass, which directly led to the rejection of your application. This high predicted risk stems primarily from a few key aspects of your academic record that stood out as the strongest influences on the model's decision. Most notably, your LSAT score was a top factor pushing the prediction toward failure, as it was significantly lower than what the model typically sees in students who succeed. Similarly, your Year 3 school decile ranking was the second biggest driver in this direction, reflecting performance well below the average for admitted students who go on to pass the bar. Your Year 1 decile also contributed notably toward this unfavorable outcome, again being in the lowest range compared to peers. On a more positive note, your low family income actually nudged the prediction slightly toward success, perhaps recognizing the resilience required to achieve under such circumstances, while your undergraduate GPA and choice to attend full-time had minimal overall impact. Compared to typical students in the dataset—who average much higher LSAT scores around 37, undergraduate GPAs near 3.2, and mid-range school deciles—your profile in these critical areas fell short of the benchmarks associated with bar exam passers. While your full-time status aligns with the vast majority of successful applicants, it wasn't enough to offset the dominant concerns. This isn't a judgment on your potential or spirit, but a data-driven assessment to help the school maintain strong bar passage rates. To move forward, we recommend retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your academic deciles through additional coursework or targeted improvement, and perhaps seeking mentorship programs tailored for underrepresented students like yourself to build a more competitive profile for future applications. You're capable of turning this around—many have—and we're rooting for your next steps.
